entry.title: Service Bindings with OAuth 2.0 for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition
entry.content:
This post outlines the procedure to specify client credentials required for SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition systems to communicate with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition, using OAuth 2.0 client authentication. OAuth 2.0 provides a secure, token-based approach that is required for newer integration scenarios with SAP BTP services.

Prerequisites

Before starting the procedure, ensure the following requirements are met:

  • Initial setup in SAP BTP has been performed manually or via the Set Up an Account for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition booster
  • Required country/region-specific processes are active for your subaccount.
  • SAP Note 3540443 – Electronic Communication Integration Framework: Implementation Overview has been implemented.
  • You have the Space Developer or Org Manager role in your SAP BTP subaccount.

Note: If initial setup for a process or business system is already complete, it does not need to be repeated for additional processes in the same subaccount region.

Step 1: Create the Service Binding

    • Navigate to the Subaccount — In the SAP BTP cockpit, go to the subaccount where the service binding will be created.

       

    • Access Instances and Subscriptions: Choose Services > Instances and Subscriptions.

 

    • Select Service Instance: Select the service instance created during the initial setup of the cloud edition.

 

    • Create Service Binding: In the Service Bindings section on the right, choose Create and fill in the fields as follows:

 

  • Finalize: Choose Create.

 

Step 2: Retrieve the OAuth 2.0 Credentials

After creating the service binding, you need to retrieve the credentials to configure the OAuth 2.0 client in your SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system.

    • Access Instances and Subscriptions — In the SAP BTP cockpit, in the navigation area, choose Services > Instances and Subscriptions.

 

    • Select Service Instance — Under Instances, choose the service instance of the cloud edition. The details screen for the service instance expands on the right side.

 

    • View Credentials — In the details screen, choose a service binding name to open the Credentials screen.

 

  • Retrieve Details — The Credentials screen displays the url, clientId, clientSecret, and drccApiUrl for this service binding.

     

Note: You need these credentials in the next steps when configuring the OAuth 2.0 client.

Summary
You have now successfully created a service binding using OAuth 2.0 client authentication in SAP BTP and retrieved the credentials needed for the integration. The next step is to use the clientId, clientSecret, url, and drccApiUrl to configure the OAuth 2.0 client in your SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system, which will enable secure communication with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.

Step 3: Downloading the Server Root Certificate

You use the server root certificate to ensure server authenticity between SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition and your business system. The server root certificate proves the authenticity of the server running the cloud edition.

Note: The steps in this section are for Chrome Browser.

  • Access the drccApiUrl: Open a browser and paste the drccApiUrl from the service binding into the address field. Your browser may display an "Unauthorized" or a "404 Not Found" message, but you can still complete the indicated steps.

     

 

  • Open Security Settings: In the address field, find the connection security settings of your browser. A dialog box with security information opens.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  • View Certificate Details: In the dialog box, look for an option related to Connection or Security and choose it. Then, look for an option related to Certificate and choose it. A new window opens with the certificate details.

  • Select Root Certificate: In the new window, search for a view that displays the certificate and its root certificates as parents in a hierarchical structure. The view might be hidden in a different tab named Details, Certification Path, Certification Authority, or something similar. Choose the server root certificate that is the top certificate in the hierarchical structure (e.g., ISRG Root X1). Export and save the server root certificate in .crt format.

Step 4: Adding a Server Root Certificate to the Certificate Trust List

Add the server root certificate to the certificate trust list of your business system so that the system trusts SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition to exchange information.

  • Access Trust Manager: In your business system, access the Trust Manager (STRUST) transaction
  • Change Mode: Switch to edit mode.
  • Select PSE: Choose the SSL Client (Anonymous) PSE.

 

  • Import Certificate: In the Certificate section, choose Import Certificate and add the server root certificate.

 

  • Update List: Choose Add to Certificate List and Save your entries.

entry.title: Shape the Future of Purchase Orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud — Join Our Customer Engagement Initiative
entry.content:
Are you working with Purchase Orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition? We want to hear from you.

SAP is launching a Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) focused on the evolution of the Purchase Order experience — and we are inviting customers and partners to be part of the journey from day one.

What is a CEI?

A Customer Engagement Initiative is SAP's way of bringing customers and partners directly into the development process — not after decisions are made, but while they are still being shaped. As a participant, you get a seat at the table: early access to concept ideas, prototypes and solution approaches, with the opportunity to give structured feedback that directly influences the SAP product roadmap.

What's This CEI About?

Purchase Orders are at the heart of procurement — and we know that the way people work with them is evolving. This initiative will look at how customers actually use the Purchase Order applications today: the processes, the challenges and the opportunities for improvement.

A particular focus will be on AI-driven enhancements and intelligent automation — we will be sharing early-stage ideas and planned innovations in this space and want your honest input on whether they address real operational needs and deliver genuine business value.

This is your chance to make sure the next generation of SAP procurement capabilities reflects how you actually work.

Why Should You Join?

  • Influence the roadmap early — your feedback shapes what gets built, not just how it is polished
  • Get a preview of what is coming — including AI use cases and modernization concepts before they go public
  • Connect directly with SAP development teams — a direct line to SAP Product Management and Development
  • Be heard — the insights gathered through this CEI will serve as direct input for SAP's product direction in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition

How to Register

Registration is open now and closes on June 19, 2026.

Register here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/4229

If you are a customer or partner working with procurement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, we would love to have you involved.

entry.title: Leveraging AI for Predictive Data Quality in SAP S/4HANA Transformations
entry.content:
Leveraging AI for Predictive Data Quality in SAP S/4HANA Transformations

Introduction

One thing I’ve learned from running large S/4HANA programs is that data quality issues almost always show up later than anyone expects. Sometimes it’s during SIT, sometimes UAT, and occasionally right before cutover — the exact moment when nobody wants surprises. By then, even small inconsistencies can trigger downstream issues and create unnecessary pressure on both business and technical teams.

Traditional validation still matters, but it’s reactive. You only find the issue after the data has already moved through part of the migration process. That’s exactly where predictive analytics and AI‑driven techniques can make a real difference.

Key Takeaways

  • Traditional validation is necessary but often reactive in large S/4HANA programs.
  • AI helps uncover recurring patterns and subtle inconsistencies that manual checks usually miss.
  • Even simple anomaly detection models can provide early warning signals.
  • Embedding predictive checks into each migration cycle reduces late‑stage rework and improves consistency.
  • The biggest benefit of AI is earlier visibility, not automation — it helps teams make better decisions sooner.

Why Traditional Validation Isn’t Enough

Most programs rely on rule‑based checks, manual reconciliation, and post‑load reviews. These work for known issues, but they don’t help you spot patterns that aren’t obvious — especially when you’re dealing with multiple regions and large data volumes.

And when the same issues keep coming back cycle after cycle, traditional validation rarely explains why.

Where AI Actually Helps

AI can highlight patterns that humans simply don’t see:

  • recurring field failures
  • strange value distributions
  • duplicate or inconsistent master data
  • unusual transactional spikes
  • region‑specific data creation issues

And you don’t need a massive AI platform. Even simple anomaly detection can give you early warning signals that help teams focus on the right areas before they become expensive problems.

Illustrative example based on practical migration validation and anomaly detection scenarios observed during enterprise SAP S/4HANA transformation programs.

Anecdote 1: The “Random” Customer Failures That Weren’t Random

On one project, we kept fixing the same customer master issues cycle after cycle. Every time, a different set of customers failed — no obvious pattern. The business team insisted the data was clean because the same records worked fine in the legacy system.

Out of curiosity, I ran a simple anomaly detection check on the historical load logs. What stood out was that all failing customers were created during a specific two‑month period by one regional team. When we dug deeper, we found they were using an outdated template missing partner function defaults.

It wasn’t a technical issue — it was a process issue.
And without the predictive check, we would have kept firefighting symptoms instead of fixing the root cause.

Anecdote 2: The Transaction Spike That Saved Us Before UAT

Before UAT on another program, our predictive checks flagged a strange spike in a specific financial posting type. The business initially dismissed it as normal month‑end activity. But the model kept flagging it, so we investigated.

It turned out that a legacy interface had been quietly duplicating certain postings whenever the source system experienced latency. The issue had existed for years, but nobody noticed because the volume was small and spread across multiple company codes.

If we had discovered this during cutover, it would have caused a reconciliation nightmare.
Catching it early allowed us to correct the data and adjust the extraction logic before UAT even started.

Why Predictive Checks Should Be Part of Every Migration Cycle

Predictive checks work best when they’re integrated into the migration lifecycle — not treated as a separate “AI experiment.”

When embedded into each cycle, predictive analytics helps teams:

  • identify recurring issues earlier
  • improve consistency across regions
  • reduce dependency on manual reconciliation
  • minimize pressure during cutover
  • track data quality trends over time

It shifts the team from reactive cleanup to proactive prevention.

Conclusion

AI won’t replace validation teams. But it will help them focus on the right areas earlier — and in S/4HANA programs, early visibility is often the difference between a smooth cutover and a stressful one.

From my experience, the real value of AI in migration isn’t automation.
It’s better decision‑making, earlier insights, and fewer surprises during the phases where surprises hurt the most.

entry.title: Persistent Class in SAP OO ABAP
entry.content:
There are four types of Classes in SAP OO ABAP:

  • Normal Class
  • Exception Class
  • Persistent Class
  • Unit Test Class

In this blog, I have provided a detailed explanation of Persistent class.

What is a Persistent Class?

  • Used to perform Database Operations Like Insert, Delete, Update.
  • Class name must start with CL.
  • While creating Persistent class two helper classes (CA – Agent Class and CB – Base Class) are created automatically.
  • When we want to go for Database Operations SAP provided a dedicated type of Class called Persistent Class. If we want to make Database Operations through SAP OO ABAP we will do it via Persistent Classes.

TCode: se24

 

 

 

 

 

We give the name of the name of the Database Table from the Menu. Give the Table name.

 

 

Now move the Table fields to the Upper View by right clicking the field and selecting CHOOSE and clicking on the  Button.

Click on SAVE Button and Click on BACK Button. We can see multiple Instance Public methods automatically created.

We have got Multiple Getter and Setter Methods. However, for the Primary key we have only Getter method and do not have Setter method.

When we activate the Persistent class SAP automatically creates two helper classes ( CA – Agent Class and CB – Base Class ).

To check the Actor Class and Base Class we can copy the name of the class and instead of CL replace it with CA and CB accordingly.

Base class is Super class of the Agent Class.

We will do Database Operations into the below table:

Insert into the Database table using Persistence class from se38:

Important Methods for Insert Operation:

1

CREATE_PERSISTENT

(Event: START-OF-SELECTION)

We will get the Object of the CL Class (Persistent Class) when we call this method CREATE_PERSISTENT of Agent class.

2

SET_<ATTRIBUTE Name>

(Event: START-OF-SELECTION)

Call the Set Method of the Attribute in the Persistent class by using the object obtained by calling the method CREATE_PERSISTENT.

3

GET_PERSISTENT

(Event: AT SELECTION-SCREEN)

This method of the Agent class is used to check if the entry exists in the table before inserting the entry into the table.

Please note:

  • We do not create the object of Agent Class to call the Instance method CREATE_PERSISTENT of Agent class.
  • In the Static Method CLASS-CONSTRUCTOR the Static Attribute AGENT will get created.
  • Also, this method CLASS-CONSTRUCTOR will get triggered only once when the Class is accessed using the Logic CLASS-NAME=>AGENT Attribute and the object will get created only once.
  • Singleton Class Concept is used here.

Steps to Insert data into the Database Table using Persistent Class:

EVENT AT-SELECTION-SCREEN:

  • Call the method GET_PERSISTENT of Agent class to check if the Entry already exists in the Database table.
  • If yes then display a Message. If No then insert the values into the database table.

EVENT START-OF-SELECTION:

  • Call the Static Attribute AGENT of the Static Class. This will trigger the Static Constructor and will create the Object of the Agent Class.
  • Call the method CREATE_PERSISTENT of the Agent class and get the Object of the Persistent Class.
  • Call the SET Methods of the attributes of the Persistent class using the Object of the Persistent class.
  • Commit to the DATABASE Using COMMIT Statement.

Delete into the Database table using Persistence class from se38:

Important Methods for Delete Operation:

S. No

Method Name

Description

1

GET_PERSISTENT

( Event : AT SELECTION-SCREEN)

This method of the Agent class is used to check if the entry exists in the table before inserting the entry into the table.

2

DELETE_PERSISTENT

( Event : START-OF-SELECTION)

We will get the delete the entry in the table by passing the Primary Key value.

EVENT AT-SELECTION-SCREEN:

  • Call the method GET_PERSISTENT of Agent class to check if the Entry already exists in the Database table.
  • If yes then delete the values from the database table. If no then display a Message that entry does not exist.

EVENT START-OF-SELECTION:

  • Call the method DELETE_PERSISTENT of the Agent class and get the Object of the Persistent Class.
  • Commit to the DATABASE Using COMMIT Statement.

UPDATE into the Database table using Persistence class from se38:

Important Methods for Update Operation:

S. No

Method Name

Description

1

GET_PERSISTENT

(Event : AT SELECTION-SCREEN)

This method of the Agent class is used to check if the entry exists in the table.

2

GET_PERSISTENT

(Event: AT SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT)

This method of the Agent class is used to check if the entry exists in the table. If Entry does not exist, then we will clear the parameter values and display a Message that Entry does not exist in the table accordingly.

3

GET_<ATTRIBUTE NAME>

(Event: AT SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT)

We will get the value of the attribute present in the database table and assign to a Local variable, and this Local variable will be assigned to the Parameter for the values to be displayed in the selection screen

4

GET_PERSISTENT

(Event : START-OF-SELECTION)

This method of the Agent class is used to check if the entry exists in the table before updating the entry into the table.

5

SET_<ATTRIBUTE NAME>

(Event: START-OF-SELECTION)

This method will set the value of the attribute into the database table

EVENT AT-SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT:

  1. Call the method GET_PERSISTENT of Agent class to check if the Entry already exists in the Database table.
  2. If yes, then call the respective GET Method of the attribute to get the value of the attribute in the database table and assign the value to a Local Variable.
  3. Then assign the Local variable values to the Parameters.

EVENT AT-SELECTION-SCREEN:

  1. Call the method GET_PERSISTENT of Agent class to check if the Entry already exists in the Database table.
  2. If no then display a Message that entry does not exist and clear the Parameter values.

EVENT START-OF-SELECTION:

  1. Call the method GET_PERSISTENT of Agent class to check if the Entry already exists in the Database table.
  2. Call the SET method of the attribute to set the value into the Database table
  3. COMMIT

Message Class in TCode se91:

Screenshot of Program in TCode se38:

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report  Z_DATABASE_PERSISTENT_1
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT  z_database_persistent_1.
DATA : lo_per_object1 TYPE REF TO zcl_persistent_3,
       lo_per_object  TYPE REF TO zcl_persistent_3,
       lo_agent  TYPE REF TO zca_persistent_3. 
" We are not declaring object   of cb class as all methods of cb class is in " ca class
DATA : lv_name      TYPE CHAR30,
       lv_ind       TYPE CHAR50,
       lv_lvl       TYPE CHAR100.
PARAMETERS : p_no   TYPE numc5 OBLIGATORY,
             p_name TYPE char30,
             p_ind  TYPE char50,
             p_lvl  TYPE char100.
PARAMETERS : p_rb1 TYPE c RADIOBUTTON GROUP r1,
             p_rb2 TYPE c RADIOBUTTON GROUP r1,
             p_rb3 TYPE c RADIOBUTTON GROUP r1.

START-OF-SELECTION.
*****************************************************************************
* Insert Section
*****************************************************************************
  IF p_rb1 = 'X'.     "Checking if Insert Radiobutton is selected
    TRY.
        lo_agent = zca_persistent_3=>agent.     "Get the object of the agent
        CALL METHOD lo_agent->create_persistent 
        "Get the Object of the Persistent Class
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_no = p_no
          RECEIVING
            result   = lo_per_object.
      CATCH cx_os_object_existing .
    ENDTRY.
*****************************************************************************
*   In this Section we Insert the values on the selection screen into the 
*   Database table
*****************************************************************************
    TRY.
        CALL METHOD lo_per_object->set_inv_name
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_name = p_name.

      CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
    ENDTRY.

    TRY.
        CALL METHOD lo_per_object->set_inv_industry
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_industry = p_ind.

      CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
    ENDTRY.

    TRY.
        CALL METHOD lo_per_object->set_inv_level
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_level = p_lvl.

      CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
    ENDTRY.
    COMMIT WORK.
    MESSAGE S002(ZMSG_PERS).          "Details Inserted Successfully
  ENDIF.                              "End of IF p_rb1 = 'X'.
*****************************************************************************
* Update Section
*****************************************************************************
  IF p_rb2 ='X'. "Update
   TRY.
        lo_agent = zca_persistent_3=>agent.     "Get the object of the agent
        CALL METHOD lo_agent->get_persistent    
        "Call the method tocheck if the entry exists in the Database table 
        "before updating
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_no = p_no
          RECEIVING
            result   = lo_per_object.
      CATCH cx_os_object_existing .
    ENDTRY.
    IF lo_per_object IS INITIAL.
      MESSAGE e001(zmsg_pers).        "Data does not exist in the table
    ENDIF.
    IF lo_per_object IS NOT INITIAL.  "If entry exists in the Database table
*****************************************************************************
*   In this Section we Insert the values on the selection screen into the 
*   Database table
*****************************************************************************
      TRY.
         CALL METHOD lo_per_object->set_inv_name
           EXPORTING
             i_inv_name = p_name.
         CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
      ENDTRY.
      TRY.
         CALL METHOD lo_per_object->set_inv_industry
           EXPORTING
             i_inv_industry = p_ind.
         CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
      ENDTRY.
      TRY.
         CALL METHOD lo_per_object->set_inv_level
           EXPORTING
             i_inv_level = p_lvl.
        CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
      ENDTRY.
      COMMIT WORK.
      MESSAGE S003(ZMSG_PERS).          "Details Inserted Successfully
    ENDIF.                              "End of Update
  ENDIF.                                "End of IF p_rb2 ='X'. "Update
*****************************************************************************
* Delete Section
*****************************************************************************
  IF p_rb3 = 'X'.
    CLEAR : lo_agent.
    lo_agent = zca_persistent_3=>agent.         "Get the object of the agent
    TRY.
        CALL METHOD lo_agent->delete_persistent 
        "Call the method to delete the Object
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_no = p_no.
      CATCH cx_os_object_not_existing .
    ENDTRY.
    COMMIT WORK.
    MESSAGE S004(ZMSG_PERS).          "Details Deleted Successfully
  ENDIF.
*****************************************************************************
* The code in the event gets trigered once the User enters data in the 
* selection screen and presses Enter
*****************************************************************************
AT SELECTION-SCREEN.
*****************************************************************************
*  Insert Section
*****************************************************************************
  IF p_rb1 = 'X'. "Insert
    CLEAR : lo_agent , lo_per_object1.
    lo_agent = zca_persistent_3=>agent.            "Get the Agent Object
    TRY.
        CALL METHOD lo_agent->get_persistent       
        "Here we check whether the record exists in the table
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_no = p_no
          RECEIVING
            result   = lo_per_object1.
      CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
    ENDTRY.
    IF lo_per_object1 IS NOT INITIAL.
      MESSAGE e000(zmsg_pers).
    ENDIF.                            "End of IF lo_per_object1 IS NOT INITIAL.
  ENDIF.                              "End of IF p_rb1 = 'X'.
*****************************************************************************
*  Update Section
*****************************************************************************
  IF p_rb2 = 'X'. " Update
    CLEAR : lo_agent , lo_per_object1.
    lo_agent = zca_persistent_3=>agent.               "Get the Agent Object
    TRY.
        CALL METHOD lo_agent->get_persistent          
        "Here we check whether the record exists in the table
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_no = p_no
          RECEIVING
            result   = lo_per_object1.
      CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
    ENDTRY.
    IF lo_per_object1 IS INITIAL.                     
    "If the Entry does not exist the table then we clear the values
      CLEAR p_name.
      CLEAR p_ind.
      CLEAR p_lvl.
      MESSAGE e001(zmsg_pers).        "Data does not exist in the table
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
*****************************************************************************
*  Delete Section
*****************************************************************************
  IF p_rb3 = 'X'. " Delete
    CLEAR : lo_agent , lo_per_object1.
    lo_agent = zca_persistent_3=>agent.               "Get the Agent Object
    TRY.
        CALL METHOD lo_agent->get_persistent          
        "Here we check whether the record exists in the table
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_no = p_no
          RECEIVING
            result   = lo_per_object1.
      CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
    ENDTRY.
    IF lo_per_object1 IS INITIAL.
      MESSAGE e001(zmsg_pers).                         
      "Data does not exist in the table
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.  "End of IF p_rb3 = 'X'. " Delete
*****************************************************************************
* This Event AT SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT gets trigerred everytime the Selection screen appears .
* For the Update Operation we fill the parameter values with the values from the database table
*****************************************************************************
  AT SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT.
   IF p_rb2 = 'X'.                       "Updte radiobutton selected
     CLEAR : lo_agent , lo_per_object1.
     lo_agent = zca_persistent_3=>agent.  "Here we get the Agent object
     TRY.
        CALL METHOD lo_agent->get_persistent 
        "Here we check if the entry exists in the table
          EXPORTING
            i_inv_no = p_no
          RECEIVING
            result   = lo_per_object1.
      CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
     ENDTRY.
     IF lo_per_object1 IS INITIAL.
       CLEAR p_name.
       CLEAR p_ind.
       CLEAR p_lvl.
       MESSAGE e001(zmsg_pers).              
       "Data does not exist in the table
     ENDIF.
*****************************************************************************
*   In this section we get the values from the Database table and assign the values to the
*   Parameter fields
*****************************************************************************
     IF lo_per_object1 IS NOT INITIAL.
       TRY.
       CALL METHOD lo_per_object1->get_inv_name
         RECEIVING
           result = lv_name.
        CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
       ENDTRY.
       TRY.
       CALL METHOD lo_per_object1->get_inv_level
         RECEIVING
           result = lv_lvl.
        CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
       ENDTRY.
       TRY.
       CALL METHOD lo_per_object1->GET_INV_INDUSTRY
         RECEIVING
           result = lv_ind.
        CATCH cx_os_object_not_found .
       ENDTRY.
*      Assigning the value in the Database table to the Parameter
       p_name  = lv_name.
       p_ind = lv_ind.
       p_lvl = lv_lvl.
     ENDIF.   "End of IF lo_per_object1 IS NOT INITIAL.
   ENDIF.

INSERT:

Insertion of already existing entry into the table:

Updation:

Non-Existing record:

Updation of Existing record:

Now update the details to be updated into the Table:

Before Updation:

Update the values in the Selection screen:

Press Execute:

After Updation:

Deletion:

Before Deletion:

After Deletion:

By this approach we can Insert, Update and Delete records from Database table using Persistent Classes.

                                                                                * End of Document *

entry.title: Exception Class in SAP OO ABAP !!!
entry.content:
There are four types of Classes in SAP OO ABAP:

  • Normal Class
  • Exception Class
  • Persistent Class
  • Unit Test Class

In this blog, I have provided a detailed explanation of Exception class.

  • Exception is a problem that arises during execution of a program.
  • This is used to raise and handle exceptions and it is raised using TRY block and caught using CATCH block.
  • The exception class must start with CX. The Custom Exception class must start with ZCX (or) ZCY.
  • CX_ROOT is the global class (parent class) for all exceptions.

There are 3 Sub classes of Parent Class CX_ROOT:

  • CX_STATIC_CHECK
  • CX_DYNAMIC_CHECK
  • CX_NO_CHECK

Explanation of Three Exception Classes:

  • CX_STATIC_CHECK:  This is checked by both Compiler and Runtime.
  • CX_DYNAMIC_CHECK: This is checked only at Runtime.
  • CX_NO_CHECK: This is not checked either at Compile time (or) at Runtime.
  • When we create Custom Exception class system by default provides CX_STATIC_CHECK as the Super class.
  • Generally, we go for CX_STATIC_CHECK. In case of arithmetic operations we can for CX_DYNAMIC_CHECK.
  • There are 2 ways to create Exception Class:
    • Exception Class without Message Class: In this scenario, we will not use the messages in the Message class in the Exception Class. But we will give our own Messages in the Exception Class.
    • Exception Class with Message Class: In this scenario, we will use the messages in the Message class in the Exception Class

TCode SE24:

Scenario 1: Exception Class without Message Class

  1. Create the Custom Exception Class
  2. The Exception ID with same name as Class will appear automatically in the Text Tab
  3. Give the Text for the Exception Id
  4. The Exception id will automatically appear as a Static Constant under the Attributes Tab
  5. In the Program in the TRY Block raise the Exception using the RAISE statement pass the Static Constant attribute.
  6. Catch the exception in CATCH block by moving into an Exception Class Object.
  7. Call the method get_text of the Exception Class and get the exception into a Variable.
  8. Display the Message

Please Note:

When we create our Own Exception Class, SAP will provide CX_STATIC_CHECK as the Super Class.

If you select the checkbox FINAL you cannot create any subclass for the Exception Class.

The above methods and attributes appear automatically from the SUPER CLASS.

By default, we will get the Exception Class name in the Exception ID (Under Text Tab).

We can give more Custom Messages. But for each message we need to give a New Exception ID.

We see only below attributes. But once we give the Message Text to the Exception ID we can see one More Attribute.

Now give the Text above.

Now you can see a New Constant Attribute under the Attribute Tab.

The text is assigned to this Constant Attribute via the value highlighted in the last column above.

We can get the Text associated with the above highlighted value using the Function Module ‘SOTR_GET_TEXT_KEY’.

We can also add a New Exception Text. Once we add we can see it under the Attributes Tab.

The New Exception Id will appear under the Attributes Tab.

When we use Exception with Super Class as CX_STATIC_CHECK and use it in the Program, when you try to do Syntax check of the program the program will give the warning ( if any ).

Table Entries:

Click on Execute:

Analysis:

  • If the value of sy-subrc is not 0 then the Exception is raised by passing the SPECIFIC Attribute name of the Exception class.
  • Then the exception is caught into the Exception Class Object.
  • Then we call the method if_message_get_text of the Exception Class and get the Message Text of that Exception and display it accordingly.

    Raise Multiple Exceptions:

    We can also raise Multiple exceptions by passing the respective Exception attribute to the Exception class.

    We can display by coding the Output in START-OF-SELECTION Event.

TCode : se38

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report  ZPROG_EXCEPTION_CLASS
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*

REPORT  ZPROG_EXCEPTION_CLASS.
PARAMETERS: p_no TYPE NUMC5.
* Data Declarations
  DATA :   LV_INV_NAME  TYPE CHAR30,
           lv_result    TYPE STRING,
           lv_result1   TYPE STRING.
DATA : lo_exception   TYPE REF TO ZCX_EXCEPTION,
       lo_exception_1 TYPE REF TO ZCX_EXCEPTION.
AT SELECTION-SCREEN.
*****************************************************************************
* Scenario1 :
* If no Entry has been made in the Selection Screen then we raise an Exception with Message "Please Enter Data"
*****************************************************************************
TRY.
* We check if no entry has been made in the Parameter field
  IF p_no IS INITIAL.
*    Raise the Exception
     RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_exception
       EXPORTING
         textid = zcx_exception=>zcx_exception_1.
*         previous =.
  ENDIF.                              "End of IF p_no IS INITIAL.
* Catch the Exception
  CATCH zcx_exception INTO lo_exception_1.
*     Here we call the method of the Exception Class and get the Text
      CALL METHOD lo_exception_1->if_message~get_text
        RECEIVING
          result = lv_result1.
*     Display the Message
      MESSAGE lv_result1 TYPE 'E'.
 ENDTRY.
*****************************************************************************
* Scenario 2 :
* If Incorrect Entry has been made in the Selection Screen then
* we raise an Exception with Message "Please enter the Correct Data"
*****************************************************************************
 TRY.
*  Select from the Database table
   SELECT SINGLE INV_NAME INTO LV_INV_NAME FROM ZDETAILS
          WHERE INV_NO = p_no.
*  Checking the value of sy-subrc
   IF sy-subrc NE 0.
*     Here we raise the Exception
      RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_exception
        EXPORTING
          textid =    zcx_exception=>zcx_exception            
      "Here we pass the Exception CLass=>Static Constant Attribute
*         previous =
          lv_num =    p_no.
   ENDIF.                              "End of IF sy-subrc NE 0
      CATCH zcx_exception INTO lo_exception.   "Here we catch the exception
*     Here we call the method of the Exception Class and get the Text
      CALL METHOD lo_exception->if_message~get_text
        RECEIVING
          result = lv_result.
*     Display the Message
      MESSAGE lv_result TYPE 'E'.
 ENDTRY.
 START-OF-SELECTION.
      WRITE : / 'The Name is ', lv_inv_name.

Execution 1 (With no Input in Selection Screen):

Execution 2 (With Incorrect Input in Selection Screen):

Execution 3 (With Correct Data in Input Screen):

Pass Dynamic Values to the Text of Exception:

  • First give an Instance attribute in Attribute tab
  • Give this Attribute as the Placeholder to the Exception Text
  • Pass the values in the TRY Block of RAISE Exception

    Give an Instance attribute in Attribute tab:

    Give this Attribute as the Placeholder to the Exception Text:

 

           

Output:

Scenario 2: Exception Class with Message of Message Class:

In this scenario we will use the messages of the Message class in Exception Class.

Steps:

  • Create the Message in TCode se91
  • Create the Exception Class in se24. Ensure that the checkbox With Message Class is Checked.
  • We can see a Button Message Text inside the Exception Class.

    Create the Message in TCode se91:

    Create the Exception Class in se24. Ensure that the checkbox With Message Class is Checked:

     

     

     

     

  •  

We can see a Button Message Text inside the Exception Class. Also, in front of the Exception Text is disabled.

 

 

 

Click on Message Text Button under the Texts:

 

Now let us create one more Exception ID.

The Exception ID appears under the Attributes Tab as a Constant Attribute.

TCode : SE38:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report  ZPROG_EXCEPTION_CLASS_MSG
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*

REPORT  ZPROG_EXCEPTION_CLASS_MSG.
* Data Declartion
  PARAMETERS : p_no TYPE NUMC5.
  DATA : lo_exception_obj TYPE REF TO zcx_exception_message.
  DATA : lv_result        TYPE STRING,
         lv_inv_name      TYPE CHAR30.
AT SELECTION-SCREEN.
****************************************************************************
*  Scenario 1 : If the Input is not given in the selection screen then we
*  display a message Please Enter Data
****************************************************************************
   TRY.
*     Checking if the Parameter is filled.
      IF p_no is INITIAL.
*        Raising the Exception
         RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_exception_message
           EXPORTING
             textid = ZCX_EXCEPTION_MESSAGE=>ZCX_EXCEPTION_MESSAGE_1.   
             "Here we pass the Exception CLass=>Static Constant Attribute
      ENDIF.
*     Catch the Exception
      CATCH zcx_exception_message INTO lo_exception_obj .
*     Here we call the method of the Exception Class and get the Text
      CALL METHOD lo_exception_obj->if_message~get_text
        RECEIVING
          result = lv_result.
*     Display the Message
      MESSAGE lv_result TYPE 'E'.
   ENDTRY.
   TRY.
****************************************************************************
*  Scenario 2 : If the Input given is Incorrect in the selection screen then
*  we display a message Please Enter Data
****************************************************************************
     IF p_no IS NOT INITIAL.
*       Select from the Database table
        SELECT SINGLE INV_NAME INTO LV_INV_NAME FROM ZDETAILS
               WHERE INV_NO = p_no.
        IF sy-subrc NE 0.
*          Raising the Exception
           RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_exception_message
             EXPORTING
               textid = ZCX_EXCEPTION_MESSAGE=>ZCX_EXCEPTION_MESSAGE
               lv_num = p_no.
        ENDIF.
     ENDIF.
*       Catch the Exception
        CATCH zcx_exception_message INTO lo_exception_obj.
*       Here we call the method of the Exception Class and get the Text
        CALL METHOD lo_exception_obj->if_message~get_text
          RECEIVING
            result = lv_result.
*       Display the Message
        MESSAGE lv_result TYPE 'E'.
   ENDTRY.
   START-OF-SELECTION.
       WRITE : / 'The Name is ', LV_INV_NAME.

Output:

Scenario 1:

 

 

 

 

Scenario 2:

Scenario 3:

This blog explains about Exception class with Message Class and without Message Class.

                                                                                    *End of Document*

entry.title: When running report /SDF/RC_START_CHECK, phase CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE fails with 'return code = 7'
entry.content:
As Support Engineer, I have seen that many customers are reporting issues during the simplification check report execution - /SDF/RC_START_CHECK.
In phase CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE, which checks for invalidated output devices, system returns 'return code = 7' for some specific output devices.
Then, customers are not sure why this occurs and which actions need to be taken.

In this blog post, I will explain why this occurs and how to solve this.

During phase CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE, access methods assigned to output devices are checked.
In S/4HANA Output Control OP2021 and higher, access methods "G", "F" and "M" are not supported.

Only the following access methods listed in SAP Note 0003057330 - "Simplification Item SI28: CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE - Check for invalidated Output Devices" are supported:

C - Direct operating system call
E - External output management system
L - Print locally using LP/LPR
S - Print using SAP protocol
U - Print using Berkeley protocol

The reason for this is when S/4HANA Output Control frame issues output for a document, it is done using a Background Remote Function call, which does not support front end printing. 

If a printer is assigned to an output request item for a document which is not assigned to one of the supported access methods, the output type will not be issued, instead it will remain in status "In Preparation". 
This was not a restriction in the past releases, however this restriction is now being enforced as of OP2021 because Frontend printing has never been supported in S/4HANA Output Control.

Therefore, customers have to customize the output devices in transaction SPAD to one of the supported access methods listed in note 0003057330.

Also, the same topic is addressed in note 0003238580:

...
4) Frontend printing is not supported by SAP S/4HANA Output Control framework (Access Methods: F - Printing On Frontend Computer / G - Frontend Printing With Control Tech.). 
... 

 
Therefore, in SPAD, for the affected output device, access method should be customized to one of the supported listed above.

This setting will help to pass phase CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE and execute report /SDF/RC_START_CHECK successfully.

entry.title: Troubleshooting the NACE/NAST Output Determination in Inventory Management
entry.content:
The BRF+ output determination framework is available for Inventory Management in S/4HANA. However, the traditional NACE/NAST output determination process still fully works and can be used in the same way as in ECC systems. When troubleshooting issues related to this output determination technology, SAP Note 426554 is an essential reference for analysis.

This blog is divided into two parts. The first part demonstrates, through a practical example, how to review and complete Checklist 1 from SAP Note 426554 to ensure that all required configurations are correctly maintained. The second part provides a detailed explanation of the system process flow, including debugging points that can help troubleshoot issues related to the classic output determination process.

Image/data in this Blog Post is from SAP internal systems, sample data, or demo systems. Any resemblance to real data is purely coincidental.

⠀

Customizing checklist #1 from SAP Note 426554

The example used here was executed on an old SAP ERP system running SAP_APPL package level 605. It consists of an initial entry of stock post on MIGO transaction using movement type 561 with an individual slip for output type WE01.

The MM-IM customizing can be reached using SPRO transaction, IMG path: 'Materials Management' → 'Inventory Management and Physical Inventory', but for short, transaction OLMB will be used to access the subfolders of that path.

1. Output type to be generated

For this example we want to use a WE01 output type with Individual Slip.

2. Form and Print program

The Form, Print Program and Form Routine assigned to the output type to be used can be checked on path OMLB → 'Output Determination' → 'Assign Forms and Programs':

  • Program: SAPM07DR
  • Form Routine: ENTRY_WE01
  • Form: WESCHEINVERS1

Transaction M706 or transaction NACE + Application 'ME', selecting the output type and then clicking on 'Processing Routines' will show the same result and can also be used as alternate paths for further information such as access sequences, condition tables, condition record key fields, etc.

3. Access Sequence and Condition Table

The standard Access Sequence range is from 0001 to 0007 and can be consulted using path OMLB → 'Output Determination'  → 'Maintain Output Types'  or transaction M706 and then double-click on the output type that you wish to check, in this case output type WE01:

Double-click on the Access Sequence number will lead us to a screen where double-clicking the folder 'Accesses' will show us our condition table (standard range from 070 to 075):

Inventory management uses B tables for the condition records, so table 72 corresponds to database table B072.

The subfolder 'Fields' corresponds to the key fields that will be used on the condition table (item 6.) and will be mapped into the communication structures KOMKBME and KOMPBME:

  • Access Sequence: 0003
  • Condition table: 72 (B072 in SE16)
  • Key fields:
    • VGART - KOMKBME - Trans./Event Type
    • WEVER - KOMKBME - Print Version
    • KZDRU - KOMPBME - Print item

4. Transaction used

We are using transaction MIGO to post the 561, on the MIGO screen we are selecting the print version 1 for the 'Individual Slip' (according to the F1 - Help for the field in MIGO):

The operation 'Goods Receipt' + 'Others' with movement type 561 assigns the transaction code 'MIGO_GI' for the header of the material document and in table T158, the transaction event type 'WA' is assigned to that transaction.

  • Transaction: MIGO_GI
  • Print Version (WEVER): 1 (Individual Slip)
  • Transaction Event Type (VGART): WA

5. Movement Type

Under the path OLMB → 'Print Control' → 'Maintain Print Indicator for Goods Receipt Documents' we can see the print item (KZDRU) assigned to the movement type 561:

This indicator can also be checked on OMJJ transaction:

  • Movement type: 561
  • Print Item (KZDRU): 1

6. Condition Record key fields

On Item 3. we found the key fields for the condition record select:

  • VGART - KOMKBME - Trans./Event Type
  • WEVER - KOMKBME - Print Version
  • KZDRU - KOMPBME - Print item

On Itens 4. and 5. we found the values for those fields:

  • VGART - KOMKBME - Trans./Event Type = WA
  • WEVER - KOMKBME - Print Version = 1
  • KZDRU - KOMPBME - Print item = 1

Now we need to check if there is a Condition Record for those values based on the Condition Table. To do this, we can use transaction NACE or path OLMB → 'Output Determination' → 'Maintain Conditions' → 'Display Condition: Inventory Mgmt' with the output type WE01:

If a condition record is found, the information we need from it is the Transmission time-spot (VSZTP) or Dispatch Time under the field 'Date/Time'.

Alternatively, this information can be found using the B table for the condition number found and then using the condition record number (KNUMH) in table NACH.

  • Transmission time-spot of the condition record (VSZTP) = 4 (Send immediately (when saving the application)).

7. Print Indicator

For the print indicator, since we are posting the movement on MIGO, the flag is marked on the header:

8. Output Record

To check the output record status, we have to open the material document posted in MIGO using the operation 'Display' → 'Material Document'  and then click on 'Display outputs':

9. Printer

When displaying the output created in MIGO transaction, you can check the communication method for the printer selected:

Debugging

1. Form DRUCK_PRUEFEN (MM07MFD0)

In this form, we check the RM07M structure for the Print Indicator (XNAPR) to confirm if  the Print Version set on MIGO (WVERS1, WVERS2 or WVERS3) needs to be saved on MKPF.  The version is updated on MKPF-WEVER field.

  IF NOT RM07M-WVERS1 IS INITIAL. 
    MKPF-WEVER = 1.
    T158-WEVER = 1.
  ENDIF.
  IF NOT RM07M-WVERS2 IS INITIAL.
    MKPF-WEVER = 2.
    T158-WEVER = 2.
  ENDIF.
[...]

In this form we also set the XNAPR flag based on the NDR parameter of the user in case the goods movement was posted via BAPI/Idoc:

* Nachlesen Parameter NDR, wenn Verarbeitung über MB_CREATE -----------*
  IF NOT XMBWL IS INITIAL.
    IF NOT PR_UNAME IS INITIAL.
      PRINT_UNAME = PR_UNAME.
    ENDIF.
    IF PR_PRINT IS INITIAL.
      SELECT SINGLE * FROM USR05 WHERE BNAME = PRINT_UNAME
                                 AND   PARID = 'NDR'.
      IF SY-SUBRC IS INITIAL.
        RM07M-XNAPR = USR05-PARVA.
      ELSE.
        CLEAR RM07M-XNAPR.
      ENDIF.
    ELSEIF PR_PRINT = 1.
      RM07M-XNAPR = X.
    ELSEIF PR_PRINT = 2.
      CLEAR RM07M-XNAPR.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
*---------

If the Print Indicator is not set, we clear the default print version so the system don't find any condition, not generating any slip. But the whole process for its determination is executed. It's not possible to skip the output determination, only to make sure it will not yield any results if not needed.

  IF RM07M-XNAPR IS INITIAL.
    CLEAR T158-WEVER.
  ENDIF.
ENDFORM.

For the example we are running, RM07M-XNAPR is set to X since we marked the flag in MIGO and the version is defined as 1 for both MKPF-WEVER and T158-WEVER because RM07M-WVERS1 is X, corresponding to version 1 (Individual Slip).

2. Form BUCHEN_NUMMERNVERGABE (MM07MFB9)

Here's where the call for the forms KOPFDIALOGSTRUKTUR_FUELLEN, POSIDIALOGSTRUKTUR_FUELLEN and MESSAGING occurs to build the communication structure for the header, item and then trigger the actual output determination, respectively:

 PERFORM kopfdialogstruktur_fuellen USING space. "<<< Build KOMKBME 

  LOOP AT xmseg.
    xindex = sy-tabix.
    xmseg-mblnr = xmkpf-mblnr.
    IF xmseg-lgpla IS INITIAL AND NOT xmseg-xblvs IS INITIAL.
      xmseg-lgpla = xmkpf-mblnr.
    ENDIF.
    IF xmseg-lfbnr IS INITIAL AND ( xmseg-kzbew = b OR t158-kzbew = b ).
      LOOP AT lfbnr WHERE zeile = xmseg-zeile.
        xmseg-lfbnr = xmkpf-mblnr.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
    MODIFY xmseg INDEX xindex.
    READ TABLE xvm07m INDEX xmseg-zeile.
    PERFORM posidialogstruktur_fuellen USING space. "<<< Build KOMPBME
    PERFORM messaging USING space. "<<< Output determination

3. Form KOPFDIALOGSTRUKTUR_FUELLEN (MM07MFN0)

Here we have the call to the function COMMUNICATION_AREA_KOMKBME from SD area to fill the communication structure KOMKMBME with the information from the header of the material document (MKPF) like the Transaction Event Type (MKPF-VGART) and the Print Version (MKPF-WEVER).

This structure is used along with KOMPBME to define the condition table based on the access sequence on SD_COND_ACCESS function later in the process.

  CALL FUNCTION 'COMMUNICATION_AREA_KOMKBME'
       EXPORTING
            MSG_MKPF     = NMKPF
            MSG_T158     = T158
            MSG_PR_UNAME = PRINT_UNAME
       TABLES
            TAB_PART = PART.

Inside the function we can check the data returned to the imported structure KOMKBME:

4. Form POSIDIALOGSTRUKTUR_FUELLEN (MM07MFN0)

Like the previous form, we call the function COMMUNICATION_AREA_KOMPBME to fill the communication structure KOMPBME with the information of the items for the material document (MSEG) like the Print Item (T156-KZDRU) from Movement Type (MSEG-BWART), Plant (MSEG-WERKS), Storage Location (MSEG-LGORT), etc.

CALL FUNCTION 'COMMUNICATION_AREA_KOMPBME'
    EXPORTING
        MSG_MSEG   = NMSEG
        MSG_T156   = T156
        MSG_VM07M  = NVM07M
        MSG_MKPF   = MKPF
    TABLES
        TAB_PART = PART.

Inside of it we can check also check the return on structure KOMPBME:

5. Form MESSAGING (MM07MFN0)

This is where the actual output determination occurs based on all the information gathered previously and stored in KOMKBME and KOMPBME structures.

The condition technique from SD area is used for the determination of the condition table based on the access sequence determined by the key fields of the selected output.

The function MESSAGING starts the process by calling Form Routine COND_ACCESS_ME (LV61BMES) since the NACE application for the inventory management output is ME.

* do condition access if form already exists
    PERFORM (L_CONDITION_FORM) IN PROGRAM SAPLV61B USING APPLIKATION
                                                        SCHEMA
                                                    IF FOUND.
* L_CONDITION_FORM = COND_ACCESS_ME

6. Form COND_ACCESS_ME (LV61BMES)

The internal tables KOMT1 e KOMT2 are filled in Form KONDITIONSVORSTEP(SAPLV61B).

KOMT1 will be filled with the output types configured for Inventory Management (KAPPL = 'ME'). The column KOZGF corresponds to the access sequence linked to the output types in column KSCHL:

KOMT2 will be filled with the condition tables for each access sequence. The correspondence in MM-IM is 1:1.

When both tables are filled, we start a nested loop on them by calling the forms XNAST_AUFBAUEN_AUS_KOMT1 (LV61BF0X) and NACHRICHTENSTATUS_SCHREIBEN (LV61BF0N).

7. Form NACHRICHTENSTATUS_SCHREIBEN (LV61BF0N)

In this form we execute the call to the condition technique function SD_COND_ACCESS:

*    start condition technique - find message type
        CALL FUNCTION 'SD_COND_ACCESS'
        EXPORTING
            application                = komt1-kappl
            condition_type             = komt1-kschl
            header_comm_area           = header_comm
            position_comm_area         = item_comm
            prestep                    = ' '
            protocol_access            = protocol_access
            read_only_one_record       = 'X'
            t682i_i                    = t682i
            koprt_i                    = koprt
            sdprothead_i               = g_sdprothead
        IMPORTING
            condition_is_purely_header = headkz
        TABLES
            condition_records          = kondtab
        EXCEPTIONS
            field_is_initial           = 8
            not_read_unqualified       = 2
            read_but_not_found         = 4.

8. Function SD_COND_ACCESS

Inside this function, we will run the form SEL_KONDTAB:

* do access
    PERFORM sel_kondtab TABLES condition_records
                        USING  t682i_i
                                application
                                condition_type
                                date
                                prestep
                                read_only_one_record
                                protocol_access
                                protocol_date
                                sdprothead_i
                                koprt_i
                                condition_in_memory_internal
                                call_modus
                                read_all_prestep
                    CHANGING condition_is_purely_header
                                condition_is_in_memory.

On that form, we loop through the key fields of the access sequence to check if they are filled. With that information, we perform a dynamic select on the condition table inside T681-KOTAB together with the key field values inside CODING_TAB internal table.

Since the select is dynamic, we do not know the exact point where it will be executed in SEL_KONDTAB form, so in order to check it we can place a breakpoint in the statement below and go from there:

IF se_memory_internal EQ no OR se_t682i-gzugr NE 'B'. "<<< Breakpoint here
    h_subrc = 4.
    IF se_prestep              = yes OR
        se_read_only_one_record = yes.
        IF t681-ksdat IS INITIAL.
        IF se_read_all_prestep IS INITIAL.
            SELECT * FROM (t681-kotab) APPENDING TABLE <cond_tab>
                    UP TO 1 ROWS
                    WHERE kappl  = se_kappl
                    AND   kschl  = se_kschl
                    AND   (coding_tab).
            h_subrc = sy-subrc.
        ELSE.
            SELECT * FROM (t681-kotab) APPENDING TABLE <cond_tab>
                    WHERE kappl  = se_kappl
                    AND   kschl  = se_kschl
                    AND   (coding_tab).
        ENDIF.

The statement below in form NACHRICHTENSTATUS_SCHREIBEN checks if the condition record number (XKNUMH) was found by the SEL_KONDTAB select (sy-subrc = 0 returned):

IF xknumh NE space.
     *nach-kschl = space.
    xsubrc = 1.
    READ TABLE xnach WITH KEY key-knumh = xknumh BINARY SEARCH.
    IF sy-subrc NE 0.

If the system found the record number, an entry is added to internal table XNAST.

9. Form BUCHEN_AUSFUEHREN (MM07MFB9)

In this form we can check XNAST table to confirm the entries built into it before the system starts the update tasks.

10.  Form PROGRAMM_AUFRUFEN (RSNAST00) (UPDATE MODE)

It is not possible to debug the print program SAPM07DR because the same is executed in update task by the call below in Form PROGRAMM_AUFRUFEN (RSNAST00), also executed in update task:

perform (tnapr-ronam) in program (tnapr-pgnam) using returncode
                                                     us_screen
                                                     if found.

For our example, TNAPR-RONAM = ENTRY_WE01 and TNAPR-PGNAM = SAPM07DR.

Those are the main points of the old output determination process run by Inventory Management, if the output is not triggered for some reason when the document is posted, those points can be useful to find out what information was missing and adjust the customizing accordingly.

⠀

Further Information

  • 426554 - Output determination checklist for goods movements
  • Output Determination in Inventory Management (IM)
  • Troubleshooting the New Output Management in Inventory Management

entry.title: From One Product to Infinite Possibilities: What AVC Can Actually Do in the Public Cloud
entry.content:
If you have only encountered AVC in passing, you might be wondering: what exactly can it handle in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition? That is the question I want to answer today — not with a feature list, but with a look at the real business processes it supports.

It starts with a model, not a product

The foundational idea behind AVC is deceptively simple: instead of maintaining a separate master record for every possible product variation, you maintain one configurable product model that captures all the rules, dependencies, and pricing logic. A bicycle manufacturer does not need two thousand SKUs for every combination of frame size, colour, gear system, and accessory package. They need one model — and AVC handles the rest.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, this approach is supported across a wide range of business processes. Let me walk through each of them.

Make-to-Order: where AVC earns its reputation

The scenario most closely associated with AVC is Make-to-Order production with variant configuration. A customer selects their configuration in a sales order. AVC validates it against the characteristic values and dependencies defined in the model, generates the correct bill of materials and routing, and passes that directly into production. No manual translation. No re-entry of requirements. What the customer ordered is exactly what manufacturing builds.

This is the core process for industrial machinery, specialty equipment, and any product where the customer's specification and the production order must match without interpretation in between. In SAP scope terms, this is the LS1 scenario — and for many companies, it is the reason they implemented AVC in the first place.

Make-to-Stock: configuration without a customer order

Not every variant business runs on individual customer orders. If you produce configurable products in advance — for example, pre-configured machines at standard option levels that you know will sell — AVC supports this through material variants. You define a specific combination of characteristics as a material variant, plan and produce against it, and sell from stock when the order arrives.

This approach works well where lead times need to be short, where demand patterns are predictable, or where a small set of popular configurations covers the majority of your sales volume. It gives you the modelling power of AVC without the wait times of pure Make-to-Order.

Engineer-to-Order: built for unique, complex orders

Engineer-to-Order with AVC is the right fit for capital equipment, infrastructure components, or any scenario where each order is genuinely unique and requires dedicated oversight. The configuration becomes the foundation for everything that follows — from procurement to delivery.

Two more processes worth knowing

AVC in the Public Edition does not stop at the two core scenarios. There are two additional processes that extend its reach further.

Solution bundling allows you to combine multiple configurable products into a single customer offer. When a customer is configuring a system made up of several independently-configurable components — a production line, a modular installation, a hardware and software bundle — solution bundling lets you manage the whole as one coherent offer rather than a collection of separate line items.

Advanced intercompany sales processing supports AVC configurations that span company codes. If a configuration entered in one legal entity needs to flow through the intercompany supply chain to a delivering entity in a different country, AVC ensures the configuration is carried correctly across that boundary — which matters both for accuracy and for pricing.

Analytics that speak the language of configuration

Configurable products generate rich operational data — which characteristic combinations are most commonly selected, which options drive the longest lead times, where production deviations occur most often. AVC in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition includes embedded analytics that surface configuration-relevant KPIs, giving product managers and operations teams the visibility they need without building custom reports from scratch.

This is one of the areas where the public cloud delivery model makes a real difference. Reporting capabilities are part of the standard scope, updated with each release, and available to every customer on the platform.

What is in scope — and what is coming

Across all these processes, AVC in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition is supported by fifteen dedicated scope items — a number that has grown steadily as SAP has continued to invest in this area with each release. The roadmap points toward further AI-driven enhancements and expanded integration with adjacent processes, and we will cover those developments in a future post in this series.

If your business deals in products that are configured rather than simply selected, AVC is worth a careful look. The question is not whether your use case is supported — the chances are good that it is. The question is where to start.

For that, I would point you to the SAP Help Portal for AVC and to the first post in this series, which covers the conceptual foundations that make all of this possible.

entry.title: Building Your First SAP Module Pool: A Step-by-Step Guide to Screen Painter & Menu Painter
entry.content:
In SAP ABAP ,Module Pool was honestly one of those topics that looked intimidating from the outside. Everyone talks about Reports and BAPIs — but nobody really walks you through building your own custom screen from scratch. So when I finally got my hands on it and saw my first custom login screen come alive inside SAP, I knew I had to document every single step. This is that documentation — turned into a guide I wish I had on day one.

What Is a Module Pool?

If you’ve worked with SAP ABAP, you’ve probably used reports and selection screens. But Module Pool programming is a whole different beast — and a powerful one. It lets you build custom, dialog-driven applications inside SAP with full control over the screen layout, navigation, menus, and user interactions.

At its core, a Module Pool program uses two key processing events:

  • PBO (Process Before Output) — Runs before a screen is displayed. Used to initialize fields, set menu status, and populate data.
  • PAI (Process After Input) — Runs after the user interacts with the screen. Used to validate inputs, handle button clicks, and navigate between screens.

In this guide, I’ll walk you through building a complete Module Pool application from scratch — including a login screen, a customized menu bar, subscreen integration, a table view, and even an F4 value help (search help).

Setting Up the Module Pool Program

Step 1: Create the Program in SE80

 

Navigate to TCode SE80, select Program, and give your program a name starting with Z (custom namespace in SAP). Right-click the program in the Object Navigator → Create → Screen.

Step 2: Create a Screen

 

Enter a 4-digit screen number like 0100, 0200, or 0300. Every screen in a Module Pool must have a unique 4-digit number.

Step 3: Set Up PBO and PAI Modules

 

In the Flow Logic tab of your screen, you’ll see the skeleton:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0100.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0100.

Uncomment these lines to activate them. Double-clicking on STATUS_0100 will let you create the PBO module and choose where to store it (use the Master Program).

Customizing the Menu Area with Menu Painter

Step 4: Set PF-STATUS and Title Bar

 

Inside your STATUS_0100 PBO module, set:

abap

SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

Both names must be in uppercase. These link your screen to a custom menu and title defined in the GUI Status editor.

Your moment here — e.g., Did this trip you up? How long did it take you to figure it out?

Step 5: Create the GUI Status (ZMENU)

 

Double-click on ZMENU to open the Menu Painter. Give it a short description and choose Normal Screen as the status type.

Step 6: Build Your Menu Bar

 

In the Menu Painter editor, you’ll see sections for:

  • Menu Bar — top-level dropdown menus
  • Application Toolbar — icon buttons below the menu bar
  • Function Keys — keyboard shortcuts

Click the dropdown next to Menu Bar, name a menu (e.g., DEMO), then double-click it to add entries. For each entry, provide a function code (e.g., SE41) and a short text label. These function codes are what PAI catches with CASE sy-ucomm.

Step 7: Add Application Toolbar Buttons

 

Click a slot in the Application Toolbar, enter a Function Code (e.g., CTRL), then double-click to set:

  • Function text: EXECUTE
  • Icon name: ICON_EXECUTE_OBJECT
  • Information text: EXECUTION

Step 8 & 9: Assign Function Keys and Save

 

 

Map your function codes to F-keys (F5, F6, etc.) from the Assign Function to Function Key dialog. Then click Save → Check → Activate.

Step 10 & 11: Configure the Standard Toolbar and Title

 

 

In the Function Keys tab, fill in the Standard Toolbar entries (BACK, CANCEL, etc.). Then double-click your ZTITLE GUI Title and enter the text that will appear in the title bar when the report runs — e.g., SCREEN PAINTER.

Designing Screens with Screen Painter

Step 12: Set Up Your Database Table

 

 

Go to TCode SE11 and either select an existing table or create a custom transparent table. In the example, two tables are used:

  • ZMP_LOGIN — stores user credentials (PERNR, PASSWORD, WERKS)
  • ZTMP_FY25 — stores customer data (Name, ID, Phone, Address, City, etc.)

Step 13: Write the ABAP Logic

Your main program handles login validation and screen navigation:

abap

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report ZTHIRU_MODULE_POOL1
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT ZTHIRU_MODULE_POOL1.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  USER_COMMAND_0100  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*

DATA: WA_LOGIN TYPE ZMP_LOGIN.

PARAMETERS: USER TYPE PERSNO,
            PASSWORD TYPE CHAR10.

DATA: CUST_NAME TYPE CHAR30,
      CUST_ID TYPE CHAR20,
      CUST_NUMBER TYPE CHAR13.
DATA: WA_RESULT TYPE ZTMP_FY25.

DATA: DOC_CAT TYPE EKKO-BSART.
DATA: IT_EKKO TYPE TABLE OF EKKO,
      wa_ekko type ekko.

CONTROLS TAB TYPE TABLEVIEW USING SCREEN 0300.

SELECT SINGLE * FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO WA_LOGIN WHERE PERNR = USER AND PASSWORD = PASSWORD.

IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
  CALL SCREEN '100'.
ELSE.
  MESSAGE 'Incorrect userid and password' type 'E'.
ENDIF.


MODULE status_0100 OUTPUT.
 SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
 SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

 case sy-ucomm.
   when 'SE41'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE41'.
   when 'SE51'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE51'.
   WHEN 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
 CALL TRANSACTION 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'CANCEL'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'DISP'.
     SELECT SINGLE * FROM ZTMP_FY25 INTO WA_RESULT WHERE NAME = CUST_NAME AND NAME = CUST_NAME AND PHONE_NO = CUST_NUMBER.
      IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
        MESSAGE 'Entered ID is not available' type 'E'.
      ENDIF.
   WHEN 'GET'.
     SELECT * FROM EKKO INTO TABLE IT_EKKO WHERE BSART = DOC_CAT.
     CALL SCREEN '300'.

 ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  USER_COMMAND_0100  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE user_command_0100 INPUT.

ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  F4_CUST_ID  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE f4_cust_id INPUT.

TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_INP,
       ID TYPE ZMP_LOGIN-pernr,
       END OF TY_INP.

DATA: LT_VAL TYPE TABLE OF TY_INP,
      LT_REL TYPE TABLE OF DDSHRETVAL,
      LS_RET TYPE DDSHRETVAL.

SELECT PERNR FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO TABLE LT_VAL .
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield               = 'CUST_ID'
      dynpprog               = sy-repid
      dynpnr                 = sy-dynnr
      VALUE_ORG              = 'S'  " 'C'
    tables
      value_tab              = LT_VAL.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
* Implement suitable error handling here
  ENDIF.
ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Module STATUS_0300 OUTPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE status_0300 OUTPUT.
 SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
 SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

 case sy-ucomm.
   when 'SE41'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE41'.
   when 'SE51'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE51'.
 WHEN 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
 CALL TRANSACTION 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'CANCEL'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'BACK'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 1.
*   WHEN 'CANCLE'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.

 ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

The STATUS_0100 PBO module handles menu-driven navigation using CASE sy-ucomm, calling transactions or other screens based on what the user clicks.

Step 14 & 15: Design Screen 0100 Layout

 

 

Click Screen 0100 → Layout to open the Screen Painter canvas. Use the Text Field element to add labels like NAME, ID, NUMBER, ADDRESS, etc. These are display-only elements — they carry labels but accept no user input.

Step 16: Add Input/Output Fields

 

 

Use the Input/Output Field element for fields where data will be shown or entered. Double-click each field and set the name to match your work area — for example, WA_RESULT-COUNTRY, WA_RESULT-ADDRESS, etc. This binds the screen field directly to your ABAP variable.

Step 17: Add a Push Button

 

Create a Push Button, name it DISPLAY, and assign a unique FCTCode (e.g., DISP). This code is caught in PAI to trigger the data fetch logic.

Step 18: Activate Everything

 

Activate all screens, GUI Status, GUI Title, and PBO modules before testing. In SE80, right-click and activate each object.

Step 19: Execute the STATUS_0100 (Direct Processing).

 

Step 20: Test

 

Enter the User & password as in the DB table

Step 21: Test

 

Enter the Values and Click the Display, the fields matching to that input will be fetched from the DB table and displayed here.

Step 22: Test

 

Now you can see the Menu area where your customized tools will be displayed.

Adding a Subscreen

Step 23 & 24: Create Screen 0200 as a Subscreen

 

 

 

Right-click the program → Create → Screen → enter 0200. In the Attributes tab, set Dynpro Type to Subscreen and save. No PBO/PAI modules need to be uncommented for a subscreen.

Step 25 & 26: Design the Subscreen Layout

 

 

Click Layout on Screen 0200. Add an input field (e.g., DOC_CAT for Document Category from the EKKO table) and a GET push button with function code GET.

Step 27: Call the Subscreen from Screen 0100

 

In Screen 0100’s Flow Logic, add the subscreen call:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0100.
  CALL SUBSCREEN SUB INCLUDING SY-REPID '0200'.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  CALL SUBSCREEN SUB.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0100.

Step 28: Add a Subscreen Area to Screen 0100’s Layout

 

 

Go back to Screen 0100’s canvas, drag a Subscreen Area element onto the screen, and name it SUB. This is the container that will render Screen 0200 inside Screen 0100.

Your thought here — Why did you choose a subscreen? What was satisfying or tricky about it?

Step 29: Test Subscreen

 

 

Execute the PBO Module SCREEN_0100 and give the input fields and check whether the sub screen is first working or not.

Adding a Table View Screen

Step 30–35: Create Screen 0300 (Normal Type)

 

 

 

 

 

 

Create a new screen numbered 0300 with Dynpro Type Normal. Uncomment both STATUS_0300 and USER_COMMAND_0300 in the Flow Logic. Double-click USER_COMMAND_0300 to create the PAI module and store it in the Master Program.

Step 36: Reuse the Same Menu and Title

 

In STATUS_0300, use the same menu and title:

abap

MODULE STATUS_0300 OUTPUT.
  SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
  SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

Handle BACK navigation with LEAVE TO SCREEN 1 (returns to the previous screen).

Step 37: Choose fields

 

Choose the fields from the table which you have already used for the subscreen_0200.

Step 38: Create IT, WA.

 

Creating the Internal table, Work area for the table .

Step 39: Write the Loop Logic

In Screen 0300’s Flow Logic, add:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0300.
  LOOP AT IT_EKKO INTO WA_EKKO WITH CONTROL TAB CURSOR TAB-CURRENT_LINE.
  ENDLOOP.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0300.
  LOOP.
  ENDLOOP.

Step 40–45: Add a Table Control

 

 

 

 

 

 

In the Screen Painter canvas for Screen 0300, select the Table Control element from the left toolbar. Draw it on the screen and name it TAB.

Use Dictionary/Program Fields (F6) from the menu bar to pull in fields from the EKKO table — select columns like Purchasing Doc, Company Code, Doc Category, Status, and Created By. Place them inside the table control and adjust column widths.

Step 46 & 47: Configure Column Fields

Double-click each column field. Under Attributes → Program, uncheck Input Field and check Output Only — since this is a display-only table. Rename each field’s Name to match your work area (e.g., WA_EKKO-EBELN, WA_EKKO-BUKRS, etc.).

 

 

 

 

 

 

Step 48: Execute

 

 

 

Process on Value Request (F4 Search Help)

Step 49: Create the F4 Module

 

Create a PAI module F4_CUST_ID that uses the standard function module F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST to provide a dropdown of valid IDs from ZMP_LOGIN:

abap

MODULE f4_cust_id INPUT.
  TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_INP,
           ID TYPE ZMP_LOGIN-pernr,
         END OF TY_INP.
  DATA: LT_VAL TYPE TABLE OF TY_INP.
  SELECT PERNR FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO TABLE LT_VAL.
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield  = 'CUST_ID'
      dynpprog  = sy-repid
      dynpnr    = sy-dynnr
      VALUE_ORG = 'S'
    TABLES
      value_tab = LT_VAL.
ENDMODULE.

 

Step 50: Add the POVR Block to Screen 0100’s Flow Logic

 

PROCESS ON VALUE-REQUEST.
  FIELD CUST_ID MODULE F4_CUST_ID.

This triggers the F4 popup when the user presses F4 on the ID field, showing a list of valid customer IDs to choose from.

Running the Program

  1. Execute STATUS_0100 with Direct Processing (F8).
  2. Enter the USER and PASSWORD credentials stored in ZMP_LOGIN.
  3. On successful login, Screen 0100 opens — showing the customer profile fields and the embedded subscreen with the Document Category input.
  4. Enter a Name, ID (use F4 for value help!), and Number, then click DISPLAY to fetch matching data from ZTMP_FY25.
  5. Enter a Document Category and click GET to navigate to Screen 0300 and view the EKKO table data in the table control.
  6. The custom DEMO menu in the menu bar lets you jump to SE41, SE51, or custom transactions directly.

 

 

 

Key Takeaways

ConceptWhat It DoesPBO ModuleRuns before screen renders; sets status, populates fieldsPAI ModuleRuns after user action; handles button clicks and navigationGUI StatusDefines menu bar, application toolbar, and function keysGUI TitleSets the screen title bar textSubscreenEmbeds one screen inside another screen areaTable ControlDisplays scrollable tabular data from an internal tablePOVRProcess On Value Request — enables F4 dropdown search helpCALL SCREENNavigates to another screen in the same programLEAVE TO SCREEN 0Exits the current screen stack back to the SAP menu

Wrapping Up

Okay, so — we built a login screen, a custom menu bar, a subscreen, a table view, and an F4 search help. From scratch. Inside SAP. Using nothing but ABAP, Screen Painter, and a lot of patience.

Honestly? It's one of the most fun things I've done in this language. There's something deeply satisfying about pressing F8 and seeing your own custom screen load up — with your menu, your buttons, your layout — inside the same SAP GUI that runs enterprise systems worldwide.

Will you hit errors along the way? Absolutely. The uppercase PF-STATUS rule will get you. The subscreen naming will confuse you for a bit. The Table Control loop logic will make you question your life choices for exactly 15 minutes. And then it'll work — and you'll feel great.

My advice: don't just read this. Open your SAP system and build it. Step by step, exactly as laid out. The muscle memory of actually doing it is worth ten times more than reading about it.

And when you're done — come back and tell me how it went. I'm serious. Drop a comment, share your output screenshot, ask questions. This stuff is more fun when we figure it out together. 🙌

entry.title: Lessons Learned from Real SAP S/4HANA Migrations: A Technical Approach to Reducing Risk
entry.content:
Lessons Learned from Real SAP S/4HANA Migrations: A Technical Approach to Reducing Risk

Data migration is one of the most underestimated workstreams in SAP S/4HANA programs, even though it consistently carries some of the highest risk. After leading multiple multi‑region S/4HANA implementations, I’ve seen how quickly data issues can impact testing, cutover stability, and ultimately business continuity. What looks like a “technical activity” on paper often becomes the deciding factor in whether a program lands smoothly or struggles during critical phases.

Key Takeaways

  • Data migration risks are predictable — inconsistent legacy structures, missing attributes, and duplicates appear across nearly every S/4HANA program.
  • Early data profiling is essential — identifying structural gaps during design prevents major SIT/UAT disruptions.
  • Automation reduces manual effort and improves accuracy — record counts, balance checks, and anomaly detection should run every cycle.
  • Multiple mock cycles are critical — two cycles rarely provide enough stability; three or more significantly reduce cutover risk.
  • Centralized monitoring accelerates issue resolution — dashboards and exception logs improve coordination during high‑pressure cutovers.
  • Real project experience matters — successful programs treat data migration as a core transformation stream, not a technical afterthought.

Recurring Technical Challenges I See Across Programs

Across industries and regions, similar technical issues tend to surface:

  • Legacy systems with different data models and inconsistent field usage
  • Mandatory S/4HANA attributes missing in legacy master data
